title: Outdoor and Campfire Cooking
content:
OUTDOOR COOKING

CAMPFIRE PRACTICES

Build fire with graduated sizes: matchstick thickness, pencil thickness, thumb size and larger. Loosely pile smallest; thrust burning match into center; add progressively larger sizes. Add fuel on downwind side; allow air circulation.

Hardwoods preferred: Oak, beech, maple, ash (then evergreens). Beech burns green; aspen will not burn.

Responsibility: Dig trench around fire area to stop root fires. Watch for sparks on surrounding vegetation. Never leave untended. When leaving: drown, mix with mud/dirt/sand, stomp—leave completely dead.

Equipment preparation: Cover pan undersurfaces with soap/detergent for easier soot removal.

BARBECUE/GRILLING

Charcoal: Use prepared hardwood briquets for uniformity. Build big enough bed to last cooking operation. Arrange 2 deep over layer of gravel. Circle additional briquets around edge to push inward as center burns down.

Ignition: When charcoal covered with fine white ash, ready to cook. Flick off ash (acts as insulation).

Heat testing: Hold hand above grill at cooking height; count "Massachusetts" or "Mississippi." Once = high; twice = medium; three times = low.

Equipment: Bellows or fan for embers; kettle for boiling water; black iron pot; skillets; hinged wire basket grills with long handles; sharp knives; metal fork with insulated handle; spatula; tongs; long pastry brush; skewers (square or oval, not round); heavy-duty aluminum foil; pot holders/asbestos mitts; serving trays; flare-up quencher.

Food preparation: Trim all excess fat from steaks/chops to reduce flare-ups. Cut through encircling sinew (without cutting meat) to prevent curling. Meat should be at 70°F before cooking. Crease grill with meat fat or vegetable oil before laying on meat.

Searing: Lower grill close to coals or use bellows to increase heat momentarily. Seal in juices before adjusting distance.

Chicken: Split birds under 2 lbs; coat both sides with oil; start cavity side down; finish fleshy side down, farther from heat.

Spit cooking: String small birds transversely; large ones head to tail. Truss if necessary; balance on skewer. Baste with butter/oil during cooking. Add barbecue sauce only last 15-20 minutes.

PIT COOKING

Dig pit 2 feet deep, 3 feet across, 4 feet long (or build above-ground rectangle of concrete blocks). Line with flat rocks (never shale). Spread loose rock layer. Build substantial hardwood fire on rocks; burn down 2+ hours. Rake out embers and top rocks. Sprinkle quart of water over hot rocks; add 2-inch layer of fresh leaves (grape, beech, pawpaw), corn husks, or seaweed. Add aromatic herbs if available.

Arrange food (fish, meat, peppers, onions, corn in husks, potatoes, squash) on bed. Add second layer of green leafage; more food; third layer of leafage. Cap with remaining hot rocks, tarpaulin/canvas cover, 4 inches earth/sand. Small pig requires about 20 minutes per pound; should test 190°F when done.

Shore dinners: Use seaweed as filler; place wire mesh over layer to support small shellfish.
